mappings.rationaleAI: African history's coverage of the Bantu expansion, medieval Sahelian empires, and European colonial penetration provides broad context across the geography and character of West African civilizations (A), pre-European kingdoms (B), and the precolonial period (C).
Portuguese Atlantic exploration directly relates to Portuguese trade with the Guinea states from c. 1460 (C.1).

The Atlantic slave trade's forced transportation of Africans via the triangular route, European trading forts, and abolition movements directly address the rise and expansion of the Atlantic slave trade (C.2).

Mansa Musa's rule of the Mali Empire at its peak, control of trans-Saharan gold and salt trade, and establishment of Timbuktu as a center of Islamic scholarship directly address the Keita dynasty of the Mali empire and Timbuktu as cultural and commercial centre (B.2.a).
The post-classical period's coverage of African civilizations and inter-civilizational trade networks provides context for West Africa until the advent of the Europeans (B).
The Ottoman Empire's role in Islamic expansion and its influence on Saharan and sub-Saharan trade networks provides context for the Islamic revolutions in the western Sudan (C.3).

The British Empire's Scramble for Africa and antislavery movements relate to European activities in the Guinea coastlands (C.4.a) and British colonial settlements including Sierra Leone, Gold Coast, Lagos, and Ashanti (C.4.b).

Nigeria's pre-colonial heritage including Hausa kingdoms, Benin Kingdom, and Sokoto Caliphate relates to West African monarchies (B.1), the Fulani and Hausa jihads (C.3), and British colonial settlements (C.4.b).
Lagos as a 15th-century Yoruba settlement and later British colonial settlement directly relates to British colonial settlements from c. 1800 (C.4.b).
Africa's overview including major empires (Mali, Songhai, Asante), Arab and European colonialism provides broad context for West African civilizations (A).
The Sahara's role as a barrier and route in African history and trans-Saharan trade relates to West African geography (A) and trade routes developed by the Dyula (B.2.b).
The early modern period's coverage of Sub-Saharan Africa and European colonization provides context for the precolonial period of European activity (C).
The Scramble for Africa's partition of the continent by European powers relates to West Africa from 1800 to c. 1885 and British colonial settlements (C.4).
European colonial expansion employing mercantilism and chartered companies relates to the precolonial period of European activity, exploration, and development of the slave trade (C).
Imperialism's extension of power over foreign territories provides context for European activities in West Africa from 1800 to c. 1885 (C.4).
Ibn Khaldun's North African historiographical framework and analysis of dynastic cycles provide analytical context for West African pre-European state development (B).
The Islamic Golden Age's scholarly networks including Timbuktu relate to the cultural and commercial importance of Timbuktu as part of the Mali empire (B.2.a).
Globalization's historical roots in the Atlantic trade system relate to the development of European slave trade routes and the Atlantic slave trade (C.2).
